title: What Is Psychology?
objective: Understand that psychology is the scientific study of mind and behaviour, learn its four major historical schools and a few landmark results, and see why the field is going through an honest reckoning about which of its famous findings actually hold up.
beginnerExplanation: Psychology is the scientific study of mind and behaviour. That word scientific matters: psychology is not just thoughtful opinions about people, it is the attempt to test claims about how we perceive, remember, feel, decide, and act by measuring real behaviour under controlled conditions. Here is one concrete result that shows the difference between common sense and evidence. In 1961 Stanley Milgram sat ordinary volunteers at a panel of switches and told them to deliver electric shocks to a stranger whenever he answered a question wrong. The shocks were fake and the stranger was an actor, but the volunteers did not know that. Common sense says almost nobody would go to the end. In fact, around 65 percent of participants pressed the final switch labelled 450 volts when an authority figure in a lab coat calmly told them to continue. Nobody predicts that number before they hear it, and that gap between what we assume about people and what they actually do is the whole reason psychology runs experiments instead of trusting intuition. The field studies everything from a single neuron firing to how a crowd behaves, but the common thread is always: make a claim, then check it against what people really do.
deeperExplanation: Psychology grew out of philosophy and physiology in the late 1800s and then split into rival schools, each betting on a different unit of explanation. Behaviourism, led by Ivan Pavlov (whose dogs learned to salivate at a bell around 1900) and later B.F. Skinner (operant conditioning, where reward and punishment shape voluntary behaviour), said: stop guessing about invisible inner states, just study the observable link between stimulus and response. Cognitive psychology pushed back from the 1950s onward, treating the mind as an information processor with memory stores, attention bottlenecks, and decision rules you can model. Psychoanalysis, Sigmund Freud's project, was historically enormous in shaping how the West talks about the unconscious, repression, and childhood, but it is largely unsupported as testable science because most of its core claims cannot be falsified. Humanistic psychology (Abraham Maslow, Carl Rogers) reacted against both Freud and the behaviourists by centring growth, meaning, and self-actualisation. A landmark of the modern era is the work of Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, who mapped the systematic biases in human judgement and popularised the idea of System 1 (fast, automatic, intuitive) versus System 2 (slow, effortful, deliberate) thinking; Kahneman won the 2002 Nobel Prize in Economics for this. Now the honest nuance, and it is the most important thing a newcomer can know. Psychology is in the middle of a replication crisis. Through the 2010s, large coordinated efforts tried to repeat celebrated findings and a worrying fraction failed: results such as ego depletion and several social-priming effects did not hold up when run again at scale. This is not a scandal that breaks the field, it is the field correcting itself in public, which is exactly what a science is supposed to do. So when you meet a famous psychology result, the mature question is not just what does it claim but how well has it replicated.
keyTakeaways: Psychology is the scientific study of mind and behaviour - the defining move is testing claims against measured behaviour, not trusting intuition (Milgram found about 65 percent of people delivered the maximum shock, a number almost nobody predicts).

Four major schools each bet on a different unit: behaviourism (Pavlov, Skinner) on observable stimulus-response, cognitive psychology on the mind as an information processor, psychoanalysis (Freud) on the unconscious, and humanistic psychology (Maslow, Rogers) on growth and meaning.

Kahneman and Tversky showed human judgement is systematically biased, splitting thinking into System 1 (fast, intuitive) and System 2 (slow, deliberate); Kahneman won the 2002 Nobel Prize in Economics.
Memory is reconstructive, not a recording - Elizabeth Loftus demonstrated how easily false memories can be planted, which has reshaped how courts treat eyewitness testimony.

The replication crisis of the 2010s is real and ongoing: several celebrated findings (for example ego depletion and some social-priming studies) failed to replicate, so always ask how well a result holds up, not just what it claims.
Freud was historically huge but largely unsupported as testable science, which is why a vivid theory and a well-evidenced one are not the same thing.
example: Eyewitness memory is the clearest place to watch psychology overturn common sense. We feel like memory is a video recording we play back, but Elizabeth Loftus ran experiments where, after people watched a filmed car crash, simply changing one verb in the question - asking how fast the cars were going when they smashed into each other versus when they hit each other - led witnesses to report higher speeds and even to falsely remember broken glass that was never in the film. In a separate line of work she showed researchers could implant an entirely fabricated childhood memory, such as being lost in a shopping centre, in a meaningful share of participants. The mechanism is that recall rebuilds an event from fragments each time, so the wording of a question, your later beliefs, and your imagination all leak into the reconstruction. This is not a quirky lab trick: it is why eyewitness identification, once treated as gold-standard courtroom evidence, is now known to be a leading factor in wrongful convictions later overturned by DNA.
commonMistakes: Confusing psychology with self-help or pop-psychology personality quizzes - the field is defined by testing claims against measured behaviour, and most viral 'what type are you' tests have no scientific basis.

Treating Freud as the centre of modern psychology - psychoanalysis was historically influential but is largely unsupported as testable science; mainstream psychology today is built on experiments, not the couch.

Assuming a famous study is settled fact - the replication crisis means several textbook findings (ego depletion, some priming effects) failed when repeated, so a result's replication record matters as much as its headline.
Believing memory works like a recording you can replay - Loftus showed it is reconstructive and easily distorted by the wording of a question or by later suggestion.
exercise: Pick one belief you hold about people - for example 'I would never obey an order I disagreed with', 'my memory of my best birthday is accurate', or 'most people can spot a liar'. Write down how a psychologist could test it: what behaviour would you measure, what would you compare it against, and what result would prove you wrong. Then look up whether a real study has tested something close to it (Milgram for obedience, Loftus for memory) and note whether the evidence matches or contradicts your intuition.

mentalModel.title: Claim, then check it against behaviour
mentalModel.summary: The core habit of psychology is refusing to settle a question about the mind by argument or introspection alone. You turn the claim into something measurable, run it on real people, and let the behaviour decide - which is why the field's most important results are usually the ones that surprised the researchers themselves.
anchors: Milgram's 65 percent obedience rate that nobody predicts in advance
System 1 fast intuition versus System 2 slow deliberation

howItWorks.title: How a psychological claim becomes knowledge
steps: Start with a specific, testable claim about mind or behaviour - 'people obey authority even against their conscience' is testable; 'the ego represses trauma' is much harder to falsify.
Operationalise it: decide exactly what you will measure (how far up the shock panel a person goes) and what you will compare it against (a condition with no authority figure present).
Run it on real participants under controlled conditions, ideally with a comparison group so you can separate the effect from chance.
Check whether the result replicates - repeat it, ideally in another lab with more people, before treating it as established rather than a one-off.

upsides: It produces findings that genuinely overturn intuition, like Milgram's obedience numbers or Loftus's false memories, which pure reasoning would never reach.
Its tools - controlled comparison, measurement, replication - travel everywhere, from designing safer aircraft cockpits to improving how doctors deliver a diagnosis.
downsides: Much of psychology studies WEIRD samples (Western, Educated, Industrialised, Rich, Democratic university students), so a result can look universal when it is actually culture-bound.
The replication crisis showed that publication pressure and flexible analysis let weak findings look strong, so a single striking study should never be taken as the final word.
teaching.workedExampleLabel: Worked example: the Stanford Prison Experiment and why caveats matter
teaching.workedExample: In 1971 Philip Zimbardo assigned student volunteers to be guards or prisoners in a mock prison in the basement of Stanford University. Within days some guards became cruel and the study was halted early after planned two weeks. For decades it was told as proof that situations turn ordinary people brutal. The honest update is that the Stanford Prison Experiment is now heavily criticised on methodological grounds: released recordings show guards were coached toward harsh behaviour, there was no control group, and the sample was tiny and self-selected. The lesson is not that situations never shape behaviour - Milgram's better-controlled obedience work suggests they do - but that a gripping story is not the same as solid evidence. This is exactly the kind of re-examination the replication crisis has forced across the whole field, and learning to hold a famous result with that scepticism is a core psychological skill.
realWorldApplications: Courtroom evidence: Loftus's memory research reshaped how lawyers and juries treat eyewitness identification, a leading cause of wrongful convictions later cleared by DNA.
Behavioural economics and public policy: Kahneman and Tversky's bias research underpins 'nudge' units in governments that redesign forms and defaults to help people decide better.
Clinical treatment: cognitive behavioural therapy, built on cognitive and behaviourist principles, is a front-line, evidence-tested treatment for anxiety and depression.

Workplace and product design: organisational psychologists use the Big Five personality model (openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism) and motivation research to shape hiring and team design.
whoWorksWithThis: Clinical psychologist - assesses and treats mental health conditions using evidence-based therapies, distinct from a psychiatrist who can prescribe medication.
Research or academic psychologist - designs and runs the experiments, and increasingly leads the replication efforts cleaning up the field.
Neuropsychologist - links brain function to behaviour, often assessing patients after stroke, injury, or disease.
Organisational psychologist - applies psychology to hiring, motivation, and team performance inside companies.

quiz.question: What single feature most distinguishes psychology from everyday opinions about people?
options: It tests claims against measured behaviour under controlled conditions
It relies mainly on Freud's theory of the unconscious
It avoids using numbers or experiments
quiz.explanation: Psychology is the scientific study of mind and behaviour. The defining move is operationalising a claim and checking it against measured behaviour, which is why surprising results like Milgram's roughly 65 percent obedience rate carry weight - they were measured, not assumed.

quiz.question: Why should you be cautious about a famous psychology finding you read in a textbook?
options: Older studies used too many participants to be reliable
The replication crisis showed several celebrated findings failed when repeated, so a result's replication record matters
Only Freudian theories were ever tested properly
quiz.explanation: Through the 2010s, coordinated replication efforts found that some well-known findings, such as ego depletion and certain social-priming effects, did not hold up when run again at scale. This is the field self-correcting in public, and it means you should ask how well a result replicates, not just what it claims.

perspectives.Clinical psychologist: I spend my days with real people in distress, not with tidy lab averages, so I lean hardest on treatments that have actually been tested - cognitive behavioural therapy for anxiety, for instance, has a solid evidence base. I am wary of any technique that sounds profound but has never been measured against a comparison group, because a client's wellbeing is on the line, not a journal citation.
perspectives.Research psychologist: My job is to design experiments that could prove me wrong, and lately a big part of that job is cleaning house. I pre-register my hypotheses so I cannot quietly change the question after seeing the data, and I value a boring result that replicates over a dazzling one that does not. The replication crisis stung, but it is the best thing to happen to the field in my career.
perspectives.Neuropsychologist: I work where mind meets tissue. When a patient comes in after a stroke or a head injury, I map which behaviours and abilities changed against which brain regions were affected. It keeps me humble about grand theories of personality - I have watched a small lesion change someone's decision-making overnight, which is a reminder that behaviour is built on biology we are still learning to read.
